key_findings: MHCII+ macrophage is one of four macrophage subsets (LYVE1+, MHCII+, TML, iron-recycling) identified in prenatal human skin (7-17 PCW)
Macrophage subsets were annotated using marker genes from Suo et al. (2022)
MHCII+ macrophages express CLEC7A, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DRA, as well as F13A1, CD163, SPP1
Lack Langerhans cell markers CD207 and CD1A
All four macrophage subsets expressed gene programs driving angiogenesis
Macrophages co-located with endothelial cells in neurovascular microenvironments ME1 and ME5
Predicted ligand-receptor interactions support reciprocal communication between macrophages and endothelial cells (e.g., CXCL8-ACKR1, CCL8-ACKR1)
Prenatal skin macrophages resembled 'early macrophages' enriched in reindeer antler skin (scarless healing model)
Identified MHC class II hi macrophages as one of eight broad macrophage groupings across prenatal organs
MHCII hi macrophage populations enriched in later stages of gestation
These subpopulations upregulated genes encoding immune effector functions
Acquisition of macrophage antigen-presentation properties (MHCII upregulation) occurred between 10-12 PCW, coinciding with adaptive lymphocyte expansion and lymph node development
Prior to this, macrophages and mast cells expressed proangiogenic and tissue morphogenesis signatures
Identified two distinct interstitial macrophage populations across tissues: LYVE1hi MHCIIlo (perivascular) and MHCIIhi LYVE1lo (nerve-associated)
MHCIIhi macrophages localized to nerves in multiple tissues including dermis
LYVE1hi macrophages associated with blood vessels
These populations occupy distinct subtissular niches with different functional profiles
Defined three conserved tissue-resident macrophage subsets across organs: TLF+ (TIM4/LYVE1/FOLR2), CCR2+, and MHCIIhi (CCR2-)
MHCIIhi population notable for lack of CCR2 expression
Different ontogenies for the three subsets: TLF+ are embryo-derived and self-renewing; MHCIIhi receive moderate contribution from adult monocytes
These subsets are conserved across heart, liver, lung, kidney, brain, and adipose tissue
MHCII expression on macrophages is differentially controlled by tissue of residence, ontogeny, and microbiota
Monocyte-derived macrophages are uniformly MHCIIhi regardless of tissue or microbial status
MHCII expression on prenatally-derived Tim4+ macrophages increases at weaning
Macrophage MHCII provides signals regulating gut CD4+ T cell maintenance
In skin, MHCII high macrophages are constitutively maintained by circulating Ly6C+ monocytes
MHCII high macrophages are primed for pro-inflammatory responses
Transition to MHC intermediate macrophages is associated with resolution of disease, tissue repair, and homeostasis
Failed restoration of the MHCII intermediate macrophage compartment is linked to loss of T cell tolerance in skin
